court: European Court of Human Rights (Second Section)

facts.text: The applicant is a Turkish national of Kurdish origin, born in 1967 and currently residing in Giresun, Turkey.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant is a Turkish national of Kurdish origin, born in 1967 and currently residing in Giresun, Turkey.

facts.text: At the material time the applicant was a judge in Tufanbeyli.

anchor.surface_text: At the material time the applicant was a judge in Tufanbeyli. On 14 August 1995 the General Command of the Gendarmerie submitted a formal complaint

facts.text: On 14 August 1995 the General Command of the Gendarmerie submitted a formal complaint to the Justice Inspection Office at the Ministry of Justice with regard to the applicant’s behaviour.

anchor.surface_text: On 14 August 1995 the General Command of the Gendarmerie submitted a formal complaint to the Justice Inspection Office at the Ministry of Justice with regard to the applicant’s behaviour. A Justice Inspector (adalet müfettişi) initiated a disciplinary investigation.

facts.text: On 1 March 1996 the Justice Inspection Office notified the applicant in writing that a disciplinary investigation had been carried out under Article 82 of Law No. 2802 into a number of acts which he had allegedly committed.

anchor.surface_text: On 1 March 1996 the Justice Inspection Office notified the applicant in writing that a disciplinary investigation had been carried out under Article 82 of Law No. 2802 into a number of acts which he had allegedly committed and which were considered incompatible with his status as a judge.

facts.text: The notification letter indicated that evidence has been obtained but did not mention the content or source of the evidence.

anchor.surface_text: The letter indicated that “evidence has been obtained” in this regard. However, there was no mention in the letter of either the content or the source of the evidence.

facts.text: The first charge accused the applicant of undermining the honour and dignity of the judiciary by introducing himself as of Kurdish origin, displaying sympathy for the PKK, talking to Kurdish speaking citizens in Kurdish, reading Özgür Ülke, and watching MedTV.

anchor.surface_text: The charges, five in all, included the accusation that the applicant had undermined the honour and dignity of the judiciary as well as respect for the applicant’s own position as a judge (the first charge).

facts.text: The other four charges related to quarrelsome behaviour with colleagues and failure to respect the dress code.

anchor.surface_text: The other four charges related inter alia to reported incidents of his quarrelsome behaviour with regard to his colleagues and to his failure to respect the dress code of the profession.

facts.text: The applicant denied the facts alleged against him.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant denied the facts alleged against him, maintaining that they were wrong, inaccurate, incomplete or had been misinterpreted.

facts.text: The applicant presumed the evidence mainly consisted of statements by colleagues and civil servants, and alleged hostility from local public prosecutor Hasan Altun and mayor Huseyin Kece.

anchor.surface_text: He presumed that the evidence against him in the file mainly consisted of statements given by his colleagues and other civil servants. He noted that in the past he had made formal complaints against the local public prosecutor, Hasan Altun, and the elected mayor (belediye başkanı), Huseyin Kece, who became hostile towards him as a result.

facts.text: The applicant admitted saying during a visit to a military command that Turkish citizens of Kurdish origin should not be suspected of being potential terrorists.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant admitted having said during a visit to a military command that all Turkish citizens of Kurdish origin should not be suspected of being potential terrorists, noting that he too was of Kurdish origin.

facts.text: The applicant admitted reading the Özgür Gündem newspaper.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant admitted reading the “Özgür Gündem” newspaper which in his opinion reported, although in a biased manner, the events in south-east Turkey.

facts.text: The applicant stated he could not watch MedTV due to technical difficulties.

anchor.surface_text: As to the allegation that he regularly watched MedTV at home, the applicant explained that he had never been able to watch the channel on account of technical difficulties with the reception.

facts.text: On 27 March 1996 the Justice Inspector drafted a report on the accusations.

anchor.surface_text: On 27 March 1996 the Justice Inspector drafted a report on the accusations made against the applicant.

facts.text: On 26 April 1996 the Minister of Justice approved the transfer of the file to the Supreme Council.

anchor.surface_text: On 26 April 1996 the Minister of Justice approved the transfer of the file to the Supreme Council of Judges and Public Prosecutors (Hakimler ve Savcılar Yüksek Kurulu; “the Supremce Council”) with a view to the consideration of possible disciplinary measures under Article 87 of Law No. 2802.

facts.text: On 11 July 1996 the Supreme Council found the applicant guilty of all five charges, imposed transfer for the first charge and reprimand for the others.

anchor.surface_text: In its majority decision of 11 July 1996, the Supreme Council found that the evidence in the file was sufficient to conclude that the applicant was guilty of all five charges.

facts.text: On 14 November 1996 the Supreme Council rejected the applicant's request to rectify its decision.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant requested the Supreme Council to rectify its decision. On 14 November 1996 the Supreme Council unanimously rejected the request, finding that its decision was correct (“yerinde”).

facts.text: On 17 April 1997 the Board for Examination of Objections rejected the applicant’s objection.

anchor.surface_text: On 17 April 1997 the Board for Examination of Objections of the Supreme Council (İtirazaı İnceleme Kurulu) rejected the applicant’s objection, finding that the decision of 11 July 1996 was correct.

facts.text: On 2 August 1997 the Supreme Council found that the applicant could not be promoted for two years due to the disciplinary transfer.

anchor.surface_text: On 2 August 1997 the Supreme Council re-assessed the applicant’s case and found that, under Article 30 § 4 of the Law No. 2802, he could not be promoted for two years as a result of the fact that he had been transferred to another jurisdiction for disciplinary reasons.

facts.text: The applicant was appointed to the Giresun area where he serves as a judge.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant was subsequently appointed to the Giresun area where he presently serves as a judge.

facts.text: The Government alleged the applicant engaged in pro-PKK propaganda, watched MedTV on balcony, tried to gain sympathy for PKK at crime scenes.

anchor.surface_text: The Government point out that the applicant, although a representative of the judiciary, engaged in pro-PKK propaganda at meetings he attended. An investigation was conducted by the Justice Inspection Office of the Ministry of Justice.

facts.text: Huseyin Kece testified that the applicant said he would join the PKK and go to Germany if dismissed.

anchor.surface_text: The witness, Huseyin Kece, testified that the applicant had told him that he would join the PKK and go to Germany if he were to be dismissed.

facts.text: The applicant had difficulties with colleagues, threatened a public prosecutor, accused him of removing files.

anchor.surface_text: Furthermore, the applicant had difficulties with his colleagues and on one occasion threatened a provincial public prosecutor that “he would bring all his tribe to the spot” if another judge persisted in signing particular documents.

facts.text: The Court found no evidence of surreptitious monitoring of the applicant's home.

anchor.surface_text: The Court observes that the applicant has not adduced any evidence to show that the authorities resorted to surreptitious monitoring of his home or collected and stored information on his television viewing habits as part of a secret surveillance strategy

facts.text: Application introduced with European Commission of Human Rights on 15 September 1997 and registered on 3 November 1997.

arguments.claim: Not disciplined solely for watching MedTV but for displaying sympathy for PKK, e.g. influencing people with Özgür Gündem articles (PKK publication), Huseyin Kece testimony; investigations and sanction justified by Article 10 §2

anchor.surface_text: The Government maintain that the applicant was not disciplined solely on account of the fact that he watched Med Tv but for displaying his sympathy for the PKK. For example, the applicant tried to influence people by relating to them the articles which he read in “Özgür Gündem”, a publication of the PKK.

arguments.claim: Huseyin Kece hostile witness due to prior report against him; no sympathy for PKK; used Kurdish as mother tongue necessary for judicial life, not support for PKK; investigation about describing self as Kurdish and reading lawful pubs, not impartiality

arguments.claim: Article 14 complaint raises serious issues of fact and law, not manifestly ill-founded under Article 35 §3

anchor.surface_text: The Court considers, in the light of the parties’ submissions, that the complaint raises serious issues of fact and law under the Convention, the determination of which should depend on an examination of the merits. The Court concludes therefore that this complaint is not manifestly ill-founded within the meaning of Article 35 § 3 of the Convention.

holdings.text: DECLARES ADMISSIBLE the applicant’s complaints that the disciplinary sanction imposed on him infringed his right to freedom of expression and that he was discriminated against in the enjoyment of that right on account of his ethnic origin; DECLARES INADMISSIBLE the remainder of the application.

a16 attacks a3 by characterizing engagement with Özgür Gündem as displaying sympathy for PKK and influencing others, countering the claim in a3 that reading it was a legitimate right to information despite bias.

a16 provides reasons for the disciplinary sanction (sympathy for PKK via Özgür Gündem and testimony), supporting the application of Article 68 of Law No. 2802 as the legal basis for the transfer sanction in the proportionality assessment.
The argument claiming no effective remedy due to lack of appeal rebuts the concept asserting availability of appeal to the Board and that the complaint is ill-founded.

anchor.surface_text: 3. The applicant further complains that there is no appeal against disciplinary punishments handed down by the Supreme Council of Judges and Public Pr

edges.explanation: The applicant's argument on no effective remedy directly addresses the issue of availability of remedy under Article 13.
Argument about hostile witnesses attacks the reliability of facts asserted in the behavioral argument
The argument that Article 6 does not apply and no appeal right is guaranteed supports the concept of available remedy via the Board.

The argument that the proceedings were disciplinary, not criminal under Article 6, with no guaranteed right of appeal under the Convention, directly grounds the holding that the Article 13 complaint is manifestly ill-founded.
The argument on disciplinary nature addresses the issue of effective remedy under Article 13.
The argument that proceedings targeted separatist views with minimal consequences (continues as judge, amnesty pardon, no promotion obstacle) supports the holding of non-criminal classification.
Argument on nature of proceedings and consequences addresses whether they constitute a criminal charge.
a24 argues authorities misconstrued criticisms due to origin and retained punishment despite amnesty, undercutting the fair or proper application of Article 82 (c9).
a3 attacks a5 by admitting the reading but arguing it does not constitute guilt, as it was a right to be informed and reading publications does not imply criminal intent.
a3 undercuts c12 by arguing that reading Özgür Gündem was a right to information and not sanctionable conduct, challenging the proportionality or foreseeability of applying the law to such acts.
a5 that evidence suffices for guilt on all charges supports the detailed argument in a16 relying on specific evidence like Huseyin Kece testimony and Özgür Gündem to show sympathy for PKK.
a5 establishes guilt based on sufficient evidence, grounding the imposition of sanction under Article 68 of Law No. 2802 (c12) as necessary response.
government argument on conduct supports ill-founded Art 8
testimony argument supports ill-founded Art 8
Concept c1 summarizing Article 8 application with no evidence of monitoring supports holding h1 of ill-founded complaint.

Article 87 provides procedural basis for Supreme Council involvement, relevant to effective remedy against its decisions
